front: Ito-Laplacian martingale for Brownian motion (9.1 Martingales)
back: If v is C2 and the stochastic integral is square-integrable, then v(B_t)-1/2 integral_0^t Delta v(B_s) ds is a continuous martingale. Exam use: The one-line engine behind harmonic functions, optional stopping, PDE uniqueness, and Green function computations. Pitfall: The integrability/localization condition is not decoration; without it the stochastic integral may only be local.

front: Mean exit time from a ball (9.1 Martingales)
back: For d-dimensional Brownian motion started at |x|<R, the hitting time S_R of the sphere of radius R has E_x S_R=(R^2-|x|^2)/d. Exam use: Use for expected lifetime in balls and as a quick check for Poisson-equation solutions. Pitfall: The dimension appears in the denominator because |B_t|^2-dt is summed over d coordinates.

front: Radial harmonic test functions (9.1 Martingales)
back: The radial functions phi(x)=log|x| in d=2 and phi(x)=|x|^(2-d) in d>=3 are harmonic off the origin. Exam use: They convert annulus hitting probabilities into algebra. Pitfall: They are singular at zero, so stop before hitting the inner radius and localize with smooth compact modifications.

front: Annulus hitting probability (9.1 Martingales)
back: For r<|x|<R, P_x(S_r<S_R)=[phi(R)-phi(x)]/[phi(R)-phi(r)], with phi radial harmonic as above. Exam use: This is the standard computation for recurrence in d=2 and transience in d>=3. Pitfall: Use the correct dimension-specific phi; the logarithmic d=2 case is a frequent source of wrong powers.

front: Two-dimensional Brownian recurrence (9.1 Martingales)
back: In d=2, Brownian motion hits every circle around the origin almost surely and visits every nonempty open set infinitely often. Exam use: Use to decide whether planar Brownian motion returns to neighborhoods, not to exact points. Pitfall: Recurrence of neighborhoods does not mean hitting a prescribed point at positive time.

front: Points are polar in dimensions at least two (9.1 Martingales)
back: For d>=2, Brownian motion does not hit a fixed point at a positive time, even when started from that point. Exam use: Prevents confusing recurrence in d=2 with point recurrence; important for punctured-domain examples. Pitfall: The definition of hitting zero uses t>0, so starting at zero is not a counterexample.

front: Transience in dimensions at least three (9.1 Martingales)
back: For d>=3, P_x(S_r<infinity)=(r/|x|)^(d-2) when |x|>r, and |B_t| tends to infinity almost surely. Exam use: Use whenever a problem asks whether Brownian motion returns to bounded sets in high dimension. Pitfall: The limit |B_t| to infinity is almost sure but does not imply monotone radial motion.

front: Dvoretsky-Erdos lower envelope test (9.1 Martingales)
back: For d>=3 and positive decreasing g, P_0(|B_t|<=g(t)sqrt(t) infinitely often) is one or zero according as integral g(t)^(d-2) dt/t diverges or converges. Exam use: Useful as a recognition result for Brownian escape rates in advanced prelim-style questions. Pitfall: It concerns infinitely often behavior near infinity, not fixed-time Gaussian tails.

front: Backward heat-equation martingale (9.2 Heat Equation)
back: If u_t=1/2 Delta u, then M_s=u(t-s,B_s) is a local martingale on 0<=s<=t. Exam use: This is the probabilistic uniqueness mechanism for the heat equation. Pitfall: The backward time t-s is essential; using t+s changes the sign.

front: Heat equation solution by Brownian expectation (9.2 Heat Equation)
back: The bounded solution with initial condition f is v(t,x)=E_x f(B_t), equivalently convolution with the Gaussian heat kernel. Exam use: Use to solve Cauchy heat problems and identify semigroup smoothing. Pitfall: Continuity at t=0 requires continuity of f; boundedness alone is not enough for the boundary condition.

front: Gaussian heat kernel smoothing (9.2 Heat Equation)
back: For bounded continuous f, v(t,x)=integral (2 pi t)^(-d/2) exp(-|x-y|^2/(2t)) f(y) dy is C1,2 for t>0. Exam use: Turns probabilistic representation into an actual classical solution. Pitfall: The smoothing is for t>0; behavior at t=0 is controlled by the initial data.

front: Duhamel representation for inhomogeneous heat equation (9.3 Inhomogeneous Heat Equation)
back: For u_t=1/2 Delta u+g with zero initial data, the bounded solution is v(t,x)=E_x integral_0^t g(B_s) ds. Exam use: Use for heat equations with source terms and for occupation-time interpretations. Pitfall: With nonzero f, add the homogeneous solution E_x f(B_t).

front: Holder source gives C1,2 inhomogeneous solution (9.3 Inhomogeneous Heat Equation)
back: If g is Holder continuous, then v(t,x)=E_x integral_0^t g(B_s) ds is C1,2 and solves the inhomogeneous heat equation. Exam use: A checklist item: representation plus Holder regularity gives a classical PDE solution. Pitfall: Continuity of g is natural, but Holder regularity is the clean sufficient condition used here.

front: Feynman-Kac formula for the heat equation with potential (9.4 Feynman-Kac Formula)
back: For u_t=1/2 Delta u+c(x)u and initial f, the bounded solution is v(t,x)=E_x[f(B_t) exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds)]. Exam use: Use for heat equations with growth/decay potential and moment-generating questions for occupation integrals. Pitfall: Check the sign convention: the chapter's plus c u corresponds to exp(+ integral c).

front: Feynman-Kac smoothness via reduction (9.4 Feynman-Kac Formula)
back: If f and c are Holder continuous, the Feynman-Kac representation is C1,2 and solves the PDE. Exam use: Use when asked to justify that a probabilistic formula is a classical solution. Pitfall: Boundedness gives a representation; regularity needs additional assumptions.

front: Dirichlet problem representation (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: For a domain G with exit time tau, any bounded harmonic solution with boundary data f must be v(x)=E_x f(B_tau). Exam use: The central Brownian representation for harmonic measure and boundary-value problems. Pitfall: The formula can fail to satisfy the boundary condition at irregular boundary points.

front: Mean-value property implies harmonicity (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: If v(x)=E_x v(B_tau_B) for every small ball B around x and v is C2, then Delta v(x)=0. Exam use: Use to move from Brownian representations to Laplace's equation without stochastic calculus. Pitfall: The ball must be compactly contained in the domain.

front: Interior smoothness of Brownian harmonic functions (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: The Brownian Dirichlet solution v(x)=E_x f(B_tau) is C-infinity inside G. Exam use: Lets you assert interior classical harmonicity even with only bounded boundary data. Pitfall: Interior smoothness says nothing by itself about boundary continuity.

front: Regular boundary point (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: A boundary point y is regular for G if Brownian motion started at y exits G immediately: P_y(tau=0)=1. Exam use: Use to decide where the Brownian Dirichlet solution attains the prescribed boundary value. Pitfall: A boundary point can be topologically present but probabilistically invisible.

front: Boundary continuity at regular points (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: If f is bounded continuous and y is a regular boundary point, then E_x f(B_tau) tends to f(y) as x in G tends to y. Exam use: This is the precise condition for the Dirichlet representation to satisfy the boundary data. Pitfall: Do not claim boundary convergence at every boundary point without checking regularity.

front: Cone condition for regularity (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: If the complement of G contains a cone with vertex y inside some small ball, then y is a regular boundary point. Exam use: A practical geometric sufficient condition for boundary regularity. Pitfall: It is sufficient, not necessary; failure of a cone condition does not automatically mean irregularity.

front: Punctured-domain boundary warning (9.5 Dirichlet Problem)
back: Thin removed sets can fail to influence Brownian exit, so a bounded harmonic representation may ignore assigned values on those sets. Exam use: Explains why Dirichlet boundary data must be paired with regularity assumptions. Pitfall: A discontinuity of the probabilistic solution at an irregular point is not a contradiction of uniqueness inside G.

front: Half-space Poisson kernel (9.5.1 Exit Distributions)
back: For the upper half-space H, the exit distribution on the boundary has density C_d y/(|x-theta|^2+y^2)^(d/2). Exam use: Use to compute Brownian exit distributions from a half-space; in d=2 it gives the Cauchy density. Pitfall: Track dimension conventions: the boundary variable theta lives in d-1 dimensions.

front: Unit-ball Poisson kernel (9.5.1 Exit Distributions)
back: For the unit ball D, E_x f(B_tau)=integral_{partial D} [(1-|x|^2)/|x-y|^d] f(y) pi(dy). Exam use: Use to solve explicit Dirichlet problems in balls. Pitfall: The surface measure pi is normalized to be a probability measure in the chapter's formula.

front: Brownian potential kernel in R^d (9.6 Green's Functions and Potential Kernels)
back: The expected occupation density of Brownian motion is the potential kernel G(x,y), proportional to |x-y|^(2-d) for d>=3 and to -log|x-y| in d=2 after domain adjustment. Exam use: Use to turn occupation-time expectations into spatial integrals. Pitfall: In recurrent dimensions the full-space occupation integral can diverge, so constants and killed domains matter.

front: Killed-domain Green function (9.6 Green's Functions and Potential Kernels)
back: For Brownian motion killed on leaving D, G_D(x,y) is the density satisfying E_x integral_0^tau f(B_t)dt = integral_D G_D(x,y)f(y)dy. Exam use: The bridge between Brownian occupation times and Poisson's equation. Pitfall: The Green function depends on the domain and boundary killing, not only on |x-y|.

front: Poisson equation representation (9.7 Poisson's Equation)
back: For 1/2 Delta u=-g in G with zero boundary data, any bounded solution must be v(x)=E_x integral_0^tau g(B_t)dt. Exam use: Use for expected occupation times, expected exit times, and source-term boundary problems. Pitfall: Sign conventions vary; here the PDE is 1/2 Delta u=-g for a positive occupation formula.

front: Expected exit time solves Poisson's equation (9.7 Poisson's Equation)
back: Taking g=1 gives v(x)=E_x tau, which solves 1/2 Delta v=-1 in G with zero boundary values at regular points. Exam use: A common prelim computation: guess radial v in a ball and verify by Laplacian and boundary values. Pitfall: Finite expectation needs domain control; unbounded domains may have infinite exit time.

front: Poisson solution vanishes at regular boundary points (9.7 Poisson's Equation)
back: If G and g are bounded and y is a regular boundary point, then v(x)=E_x integral_0^tau g(B_t)dt tends to zero as x tends to y from inside G. Exam use: Use to verify the zero boundary condition for occupation-time solutions. Pitfall: Regularity and boundedness assumptions are doing real work here.

front: Half-space Green function by reflection (9.7.1 Occupation Times)
back: For the half-space, the killed Green function equals the full-space potential at y minus the full-space potential at the reflected point y_bar. Exam use: Compute occupation times before exiting a half-space. Pitfall: The reflected term has the same singularity mirrored across the boundary, not at the original y.

front: Ball Green function by Kelvin transform (9.7.1 Occupation Times)
back: For the unit ball in d>=3, G_D(x,y)=G(x,y)-|y|^(2-d)G(x,y/|y|^2); a logarithmic analog holds in d=2. Exam use: Use for explicit occupation densities in balls. Pitfall: The formula has a singular-looking y=0 case that must be handled by a limit or separately.

front: Schrodinger boundary-value martingale (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: For 1/2 Delta u+c(x)u=0 in G, u(B_t) exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds) is a local martingale up to the exit time. Exam use: Foundation for boundary Feynman-Kac formulas and exponential exit-time moments. Pitfall: The sign of c controls integrability; positive c can make the expectation infinite.

front: Positive potential can destroy bounded representation (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: For G=(-a,a), c=gamma>0, and boundary value 1, the ODE solution exists only away from eigenvalue obstructions and exponential exit moments can blow up. Exam use: Use as a warning that Feynman-Kac with positive potential needs integrability assumptions. Pitfall: Bounded c does not automatically imply E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds) is finite.

front: Gauge function for exponential lifetime integrability (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: The gauge w(x)=E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds) measures whether the Schrodinger Feynman-Kac representation is finite. Exam use: Check this before asserting uniqueness or boundary representations with exponential weights. Pitfall: Finiteness at one point and boundedness over the domain require separate hypotheses in the chapter.

front: Small-set exponential exit bound (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: For each theta>0, sufficiently small open sets H have uniformly bounded E_x exp(theta tau_H). Exam use: Technical tool that makes the gauge theorem work. Pitfall: Small Lebesgue measure is the key condition, not merely small diameter in the statement.

front: Gauge finiteness propagates on connected domains (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: If G is connected and the gauge w is not identically infinite, then w(x)<infinity for every x in G; if |G|<infinity, w is bounded. Exam use: Use to justify uniform integrability in Schrodinger uniqueness proofs. Pitfall: Connectedness matters; separate components need separate checks.

front: Schrodinger Dirichlet representation (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: Under bounded connected domain, bounded continuous f and c, and finite gauge, the bounded solution is v(x)=E_x[f(B_tau) exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds)]. Exam use: Use for boundary-value problems with killing or creation potentials. Pitfall: Without the finite-gauge assumption, the formula may be infinite or uniqueness may fail.

front: Schrodinger boundary condition at regular points (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: At regular boundary points, the Schrodinger representation tends to the boundary value f(y). Exam use: Use to finish verification of boundary data for the weighted representation. Pitfall: The exponential weight adds a tail-control requirement absent from the plain Dirichlet problem.

front: Holder potential gives C2 Schrodinger solution (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: With the chapter's bounded-domain/gauge assumptions and Holder continuous c, the Schrodinger representation is C2 and solves 1/2 Delta v+c v=0. Exam use: Use to upgrade the probabilistic representation to a classical solution. Pitfall: Smoothness is interior; boundary regularity is a separate issue.

front: Exponential exit moment from an interval (9.8 Schrodinger Equation)
back: For tau exiting (-a,a), E_x exp(gamma tau)=cos(x sqrt(2 gamma))/cos(a sqrt(2 gamma)) when 0<gamma<pi^2/(8a^2), and is infinite at or above the threshold. Exam use: A high-yield explicit computation connecting Brownian exit times with eigenvalues. Pitfall: The threshold is the first Dirichlet eigenvalue for the interval; at the threshold the denominator vanishes.

front: Backward heat martingale template (9.2 Exercises: Heat equation, Brownian semigroup, and heat-kernel smoothing)
back: For u_t=(1/2)Delta u, apply Ito to u(t-s,B_s); the time derivative cancels the Brownian drift and leaves a local martingale. Exam use: Section 9.2 martingale checks and any backward parabolic uniqueness argument. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Bounded heat solution uniqueness by terminal martingale (9.2 Exercises: Heat equation, Brownian semigroup, and heat-kernel smoothing)
back: Boundedness upgrades the backward local martingale to a uniformly integrable martingale; sending s up to t identifies u(t,x) with E_x f(B_t). Exam use: Heat equation uniqueness and bounded parabolic Cauchy problems. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Gaussian approximate identity at t=0 (9.2 Exercises: Heat equation, Brownian semigroup, and heat-kernel smoothing)
back: Write B_t as sqrt(t)Z; bounded convergence proves E f(x_n+sqrt(t_n)Z) tends to f(x) when f is bounded continuous. Exam use: Initial-condition verification for heat and Feynman-Kac formulas. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Heat-kernel derivative majorants (9.2 Exercises: Heat equation, Brownian semigroup, and heat-kernel smoothing)
back: On t bounded away from zero, derivatives of the Gaussian kernel are polynomial times a Gaussian, giving integrable majorants for differentiating under the integral. Exam use: Proving C^{1,2} smoothing for heat semigroups. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Source-term martingale for inhomogeneous heat equations (9.3 Exercises: Inhomogeneous heat equation and Duhamel occupation formulas)
back: For u_t=(1/2)Delta u+g, add integral_0^s g(B_r)dr to u(t-s,B_s) so the drift cancels. Exam use: Duhamel representation and source-term uniqueness. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Duhamel uniqueness from bounded martingales (9.3 Exercises: Inhomogeneous heat equation and Duhamel occupation formulas)
back: A bounded inhomogeneous heat solution with zero initial data equals E_x integral_0^t g(B_s)ds by stopping the source-term martingale at s=t. Exam use: Identifying source-forced heat solutions and occupation integrals. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Short-time Markov-property PDE verification (9.3 Exercises: Inhomogeneous heat equation and Duhamel occupation formulas)
back: Use v(t+h,x)=E_x[v(t,B_h)+short-time reward], Taylor expand v(t,B_h), divide by h, and let h down to zero. Exam use: Showing smooth Brownian representations solve heat, Poisson, and Feynman-Kac PDEs. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Holder cancellation for singular heat-source kernels (9.3 Exercises: Inhomogeneous heat equation and Duhamel occupation formulas)
back: Subtract g(x) inside P_s g(x)-g(x); Holder continuity gives O(s^{alpha/2}), offsetting the small-time singularity. Exam use: Regularity of inhomogeneous heat and potential-kernel formulas. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Exponential potential weight martingale (9.4 Exercises: Feynman-Kac formula for heat equations with potentials)
back: Multiply by exp(integral c(B_s)ds); the finite-variation product rule adds exactly the c u drift needed for cancellation. Exam use: Feynman-Kac and Schrodinger martingale derivations. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Bounded Feynman-Kac uniqueness (9.4 Exercises: Feynman-Kac formula for heat equations with potentials)
back: If c and u are bounded on finite time intervals, the weighted martingale is bounded; stopping at t gives u(t,x)=E_x[f(B_t)exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds)]. Exam use: Parabolic PDEs with bounded multiplicative potentials. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Smooth Feynman-Kac short-time check (9.4 Exercises: Feynman-Kac formula for heat equations with potentials)
back: Use v(t+h,x)=E_x[e^{int_0^h c(B_s)ds}v(t,B_h)], then expand the Brownian move and the exponential to get v_t=(1/2)Delta v+c v. Exam use: Verifying smooth Feynman-Kac representations solve the PDE. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Feynman-Kac smoothness by Duhamel reduction (9.4 Exercises: Feynman-Kac formula for heat equations with potentials)
back: Use e^{A_t}-1=integral_0^t c(B_s)e^{A_t-A_s}ds to decompose v into a heat solution plus an inhomogeneous heat solution with source c v. Exam use: Regularity proofs for Feynman-Kac formulas with Holder f and c. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Liouville theorem via heat semigroup flattening (9.5 Exercises: Dirichlet problem, Liouville principles, and boundary regularity)
back: A bounded harmonic h satisfies h(x)=E h(x+B_t); as t grows, two Gaussian laws with fixed mean separation have vanishing total variation distance, so h(x)=h(y). Exam use: Bounded harmonic functions on all of R^d. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Recurrent Brownian motion forces nonnegative superharmonic functions constant (9.5 Exercises: Dirichlet problem, Liouville principles, and boundary regularity)
back: Nonnegative superharmonic functions yield supermartingales; in d=1,2 Brownian motion hits every small neighborhood, so optional stopping gives f(x)>=f(y) and symmetry gives equality. Exam use: Superharmonic Liouville-type exercises in recurrent dimensions. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Radial superharmonic examples in high dimension (9.5 Exercises: Dirichlet problem, Liouville principles, and boundary regularity)
back: Use the radial Laplacian g''(r)+(d-1)g'(r)/r; functions such as (1+r^2)^{-(d-2)/2} are smooth nonconstant nonnegative superharmonic for d>=3. Exam use: Constructing counterexamples to recurrent-dimensional constancy in transient dimensions. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Escape probability is the nonuniqueness direction (9.5 Exercises: Dirichlet problem, Liouville principles, and boundary regularity)
back: When Brownian motion can avoid exiting G, q(x)=P_x(tau=infinity) is a bounded zero-boundary harmonic function; all bounded zero-boundary solutions differ by c q. Exam use: Dirichlet problems in domains where exit is not almost sure. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Flat cone boundary regularity (9.5 Exercises: Dirichlet problem, Liouville principles, and boundary regularity)
back: Use zeros of the perpendicular Brownian coordinate and the induced Cauchy trace on the hyperplane to show Brownian motion immediately hits a flat exterior cone. Exam use: Checking regular boundary points under weaker geometric exterior conditions. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Infinite ball occupation in recurrent dimensions (9.6 Exercises: Green functions, occupation densities, and potential-kernel normalization)
back: Use recurrence to get infinitely many returns to a ball and the strong Markov property to sum positive occupation increments. Exam use: Occupation-time dichotomy for Brownian motion in d<=2. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Gamma change of variables for transient potential kernels (9.6 Exercises: Green functions, occupation densities, and potential-kernel normalization)
back: For d>=3, integrate the heat kernel over time and use s=|x-y|^2/(2t) to get Gamma(d/2-1)/(2 pi^{d/2}) |x-y|^{2-d}. Exam use: Green-function and expected occupation-density calculations. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Modified potential kernels in recurrent dimensions (9.6 Exercises: Green functions, occupation densities, and potential-kernel normalization)
back: Subtract a reference heat kernel before integrating over time; in d=1 this gives -|x-y| and in d=2 gives -(1/pi)log|x-y|. Exam use: Using potential kernels when the full-space occupation integral diverges. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Frullani integral for the two-dimensional log kernel (9.6 Exercises: Green functions, occupation densities, and potential-kernel normalization)
back: After u=1/(2t), the difference of two planar heat kernels becomes integral_0^infty (e^{-a^2u}-e^{-u})du/u=-2log a. Exam use: Deriving the d=2 logarithmic potential kernel. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Distributional delta normalization of Green kernels (9.6 Exercises: Green functions, occupation densities, and potential-kernel normalization)
back: Integrate by parts outside a small ball and let the radius shrink; the boundary flux fixes the constant so (1/2)Delta G=-delta_0. Exam use: Checking Green-function constants and fundamental solutions. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Exit-time moment recursion (9.7 Exercises: Poisson equation and exit-time moment recursions)
back: For m_n(x)=E_x tau^n, the Markov property or Dynkin formula gives (1/2)Delta m_n=-n m_{n-1} with zero boundary values. Exam use: Computing higher moments of Brownian exit times. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Second exit-time moment Poisson system (9.7 Exercises: Poisson equation and exit-time moment recursions)
back: First solve (1/2)Delta v=-1 for v=E tau; then solve (1/2)Delta w=-2v for w=E tau^2, both with zero boundary values. Exam use: Exercise 9.7.1 and second-moment exit-time computations. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Schrodinger weighted martingale (9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments)
back: For (1/2)Delta u+c u=0, Ito plus the finite-variation product rule makes u(B_t)exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds) a local martingale before exit. Exam use: Boundary Feynman-Kac formulas for elliptic equations with potential. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Gauge integrability condition (9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments)
back: Before applying boundary Feynman-Kac with positive potential, check w(x)=E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds); bounded c alone does not ensure finiteness. Exam use: Avoiding false Schrodinger Feynman-Kac representations. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Interval eigenvalue threshold for exponential exit times (9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments)
back: For tau exiting (-a,a), solve (1/2)u''+gamma u=0 with boundary value one; finiteness holds exactly below gamma=pi^2/(8a^2). Exam use: Explicit exponential exit-time moments and gauge blow-up examples. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Finite-gauge boundary Feynman-Kac uniqueness (9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments)
back: Finite bounded gauge controls the terminal exponential weight and kills the tail term, giving u(x)=E_x[f(B_tau)exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds)]. Exam use: Uniqueness of bounded Schrodinger Dirichlet solutions. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

front: Local exit expansion for smooth Schrodinger representations (9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments)
back: Stop at a small ball, expand v(B_sigma) and exp(integral_0^sigma c(B_s)ds), then divide by E sigma to get (1/2)Delta v+c v=0. Exam use: Verifying smooth Schrodinger Feynman-Kac formulas solve the PDE. Pitfall: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.
